metadata.summary: Maps BA competencies to agile frameworks (Scrum, SAFe, Disciplined Agile, IIBA Agile Extension) so an analyst can choose a canonical framework, audit adherence against the IIBA seven principles, and generate a framework-fit report.

content.title: Agile BA Frameworks
content.summary: Maps BA competencies to agile frameworks (Scrum, SAFe, Disciplined Agile, IIBA Agile Extension) so an analyst can choose a canonical framework, audit adherence against the IIBA seven principles, and generate a framework-fit report. Distinct from the operational sprint-cadence BA work; this layer covers framework selection and constitution-level decisions.

why.p: 71% of BAs practice agile yet scaled agile frameworks do not define an explicit BA role. Without a principled framework selection, teams conflate ceremonies with practices and organisations repeatedly litigate the same "where does the BA fit?" question. IIBA Agile Extension v2's seven principles provide an auditable checklist; Disciplined Agile supplies lifecycle-choice tooling; both are testable against project evidence.

list.item: Picking an agile BA framework for a new initiative before the team commits to Scrum/SAFe/DA.
Auditing an existing delivery against IIBA Agile Extension's seven principles to produce a gap report.
Mapping BABOK v3 knowledge areas onto an agile horizon when a BA function migrates from waterfall.
Choosing between Disciplined Agile lifecycles using the DA "Way of Working" decision tree.
Writing a constitution or playbook section that codifies "how we do agile BA" with official source references.
Preparing IIBA Agile Analysis Certification (AAC) study material or competency self-assessment.
Day-to-day backlog refinement and sprint-cadence artifact generation — use the business-analyst variant.
Single-team Scrum with no scaling concern and no regulatory traceability need — overhead is not justified.
Greenfield product discovery — use continuous-discovery, user-story-mapping, strategy-analysis instead.
Non-software domains (marketing ops, HR change) — frameworks lean software; misapplied vocabulary creates false precision.
Pure tooling questions (Jira API, Linear webhooks) — frameworks here are vendor-neutral.

rule.statement: Assign specific BA deliverables to each Scrum phase; do not treat BA work as a single undifferentiated "refinement" activity.
rule.rationale: BA activities that are not assigned to a ceremony are skipped or performed ad-hoc, causing acceptance-criteria defects to surface only at Sprint Review.

example.title: BA activities mapped to Scrum ceremonies
example.note: Sprint 0: vision, roadmap, initial backlog creation. Refinement: user story elaboration, acceptance criteria, models. Planning: story clarification, dependency identification. Execution: ad-hoc clarification under 30 min response target. Review: solution validation, feedback capture. Retrospective: BA process improvement.

rule.statement: Map the BA role to the SAFe level before the PI Planning ceremony; do not assume a single BA role covers all levels.
rule.rationale: SAFe does not define an explicit BA role; without explicit mapping the BA defaults to Team Level tasks even when the initiative requires Program or Portfolio BA work.

example.title: BA role mapping across SAFe levels
example.note: Team Level: Product Owner duties — user stories, acceptance criteria, backlog management. Program Level: Senior BA / System Architect — domain modeling, feature analysis, dependency analysis. Large Solution: Solution Analyst — cross-ART coordination, capability definition. Portfolio Level: Enterprise BA — portfolio backlog grooming, strategic alignment.

list.item: Bridge business and IT: translate business needs to technical requirements.
Facilitate communication: enable collaboration among stakeholders across cadences.
Prioritize by value: support value-based feature prioritization at each level.
Define vision and roadmap: support Product Management in strategic direction.
Ensure alignment: verify agile delivery aligns with business goals at each PI.

rule.statement: Select agile BA technique by purpose, not by familiarity; each technique has a distinct trigger.
rule.rationale: Using a single technique (e.g. user stories) for all purposes leaves discovery gaps that surface as missed requirements after Sprint Review.

example.title: Agile BA techniques and their purpose
example.note: User Story Mapping: release planning, feature prioritization. Example Mapping: acceptance criteria clarification. Story Splitting: breaking epics into implementable stories. Impact Mapping: connecting features to business goals. Event Storming: domain discovery and process modeling.

rule.statement: Score each of the seven IIBA Agile Extension principles with a quoted evidence line; reject any score row that lacks an evidence quote.
rule.rationale: Principle scoring without evidence reduces to opinion; only cited artefacts survive a CCBA/CBAP audit and provide actionable gap data.

list.item: See the Whole
Think as a Customer
Analyze to Determine What is Valuable
Get Real Using Examples
Understand What is Doable
Stimulate Collaboration and Continuous Improvement
Avoid Waste

example.title: Scoring without evidence
example.note: Scoring "Principle 4: 4/5" with no quoted evidence. Agents routinely produce scores without source anchors, making the audit unverifiable.

rule.statement: Choose one canonical framework as the anchor and label techniques borrowed from others explicitly; do not mix IIBA AE + DA + SAFe vocabulary without declaring the anchor.
rule.rationale: Mixed vocabulary creates false precision and forces future BAs to relitigate framework choices every sprint.

example.title: Framework selection by team scale
example.note: IIBA Agile Extension: scope single team to 2-3 teams, low adoption cost, principles-based. Disciplined Agile: mid-org mixed cadences, 6 lifecycle options, explicit BA roles. SAFe: large enterprise 50+ people, highest adoption cost, strongest scaling guidance. Do not propose SAFe for teams under 50 people.

antipattern.description: Treating ceremony coverage as principle adherence — "we run stand-ups therefore we Stimulate Collaboration."
antipattern.reason: Ceremonies are necessary but not sufficient; principle adherence requires evidence of behaviour change, not attendance records.

rule.statement: Select a single DA primary lifecycle and at most one alternative; reject "all lifecycles apply" outputs.
rule.rationale: DA documents 6 lifecycles; an agent given all options will select all, making the choice useless for team planning.

list.item: Agile — iterative, team-scoped, low process weight.
Lean — flow-optimised, WIP-limited, continuous improvement.
Continuous Delivery: Agile — CD pipeline required, assumes mature DevOps.
Continuous Delivery: Lean — CD + Lean, highest DevOps maturity bar.
Exploratory — R&D, proof-of-concept, unknown solution space.
Program — multi-team coordination, large solution scope.

rule.statement: Run the framework fit as a knowledge-retrieval and synthesis pipeline, not a periodic cron; trigger is one-shot on initiative setup or after a major team/scope change.
rule.rationale: Framework choices are not daily tasks; automating them on a schedule produces stale recommendations that erode trust in the analysis.

list.item: Step 1: Ingest team state (constitution, roadmap, sample backlog, retro notes, regulatory profile).
Step 2: Score IIBA AE seven principles (1-5, with one quoted evidence line per score).
Step 3: Run DA lifecycle fit scoring against team context dimensions (size, DevOps maturity, discovery maturity).
Step 4: Emit framework-fit.md: primary framework, secondary alternative, top 3 principle gaps with concrete remediation actions.
Step 5: Hand off operational work (story refinement, AC generation) to the business-analyst variant agent.

rule.statement: Pin framework versions in the constitution; refresh the framework-fit report after major team or scope changes.
rule.rationale: Without version pins, agent output drifts as upstream content updates; without refresh triggers, recommendations become stale after M&A, new regulatory load, or team size doubling.

list.item: Pin framework versions in the constitution. "IIBA Agile Extension v2 (2017), Disciplined Agile via PMI Browser as of <quarter>." Without pin, agent output drifts as upstream content updates.
Treat the seven IIBA principles as audit checklist, not aspiration. Score quarterly with quoted evidence; principle scores < 3 become explicit backlog items, not "we'll do better."
Choose one canonical framework, supplement with techniques from others. Mixing DA + SAFe + IIBA AE without an anchor produces vocabulary chaos. Pick the anchor, label the borrowed techniques.
Cite primary sources, never blog summaries. Agents trained on aggregator content reproduce errors. Prompt explicitly: "cite IIBA, PMI, Scaled Agile only."
Decouple framework selection from tooling selection. Choosing DA does not pick Jira; choosing Linear does not pick Scrum. Agents that conflate them produce false constraints.
Run principle audit before framework switch, not after. Switching frameworks to fix a principle gap usually fails — the gap is cultural. Address principles first; framework follows.
Document explicit non-adoptions. "We do not adopt SAFe Portfolio level — we have one product." Saves future agents from re-litigating.
Refresh the framework-fit report after major team or scope changes. Triggers: team size doubles, new regulatory load, M&A, new product line.

antipattern.reason: Disciplined Agile has ~24 process goals; agents under context pressure invent plausible-sounding extras. Force the agent to enumerate from a vetted list — fail closed if it can't.

antipattern.description: BABOK v3 quoted into agile contexts verbatim.
antipattern.reason: Knowledge-area definitions are waterfall-tinted; quoting them as agile guidance misleads. Always cross-reference with the Agile Extension first.
Models hand back "Principle 4: 4/5" with no quote. Reject any score row missing a quoted source line.
Agents pattern-match on "agile + scaling" → SAFe regardless of fit. Add a guard: "if team < 50 people, do not propose SAFe."
When given 6 lifecycle options the agent picks "all of them apply" — useless. Force a single primary + at most one alternative.
Agents reproduce 2017 competency wording in 2025 contexts. Pin the AAC version in system prompt or grading is dated.
Anthropic-trained models lean toward Atlassian / SAFe content (over-represented online). When asked about DA or IIBA, output is thinner — boost via explicit reference packs.
Constitution is the single source of truth; agent must propose diffs, never write directly. Always review-then-merge.

antipattern.description: Citation of paywalled IIBA / PMI content.
antipattern.reason: Some agents quote BABOK passages verbatim. License risk. Restrict to summary + URL; never inline long quotes.

antipattern.description: Confusing Disciplined Agile (PMI) with Disciplined Agile Delivery (Scott Ambler, pre-2019).
antipattern.reason: Different scope, different vocabulary. Pin the post-2019 PMI version unless explicitly using the legacy material.

template.purpose: Framework comparison matrix and IIBA seven-principles scorecard skeleton.
Per-ceremony BA activity checklist for Scrum sprints.
